_doc: STRUCTURED CORE — the distillation of policy.raw.md into machine-adjudicable form. This is the 'expert's internalized rulebook': entities, rules, exceptions, and the precedence order that resolves conflicts. The reference examiner (engine.mjs) runs on THIS; candidate models read only policy.raw.md and must reconstruct this reasoning themselves. Synthetic. 'Meridian Mutual' is fictional; nothing here is real insurance or legal advice.

deductibles._note: The wind/hail deductible applies to any loss whose proximate covered peril is windstorm or hail. Otherwise the standard deductible applies. Deductible is subtracted from the covered loss amount AFTER sub-limits, never before.

exclusions.label: Wear, tear, deterioration, lack of maintenance, rust, corrosion
Mold, fungus, wet or dry rot
Intentional or fraudulent act by an insured
Continuous or repeated seepage/leakage over 14+ days
Loss while the dwelling was vacant more than 60 consecutive days before the loss

rules.text: A loss is payable only if its proximate cause is a covered peril.
If an excluded peril contributes to the loss, the loss is excluded EVEN IF a covered peril also contributes (concurrent causation). This is overridden only by R3.

If an excluded cause (only one of: wear_and_tear, mold) leads to a SEPARATE ensuing covered peril, the ensuing loss IS covered, but the cost to repair the excluded cause itself is NOT. Flood, earth_movement, intentional_act, seepage, and vacancy are NOT ensuing-loss eligible.
Payable loss for a sub-limited category is capped at its sub-limit before the deductible is applied.
Subtract the applicable deductible from the covered, sub-limit-capped amount. If the result is <= 0, the claim is payable-but-below-deductible (net pay = 0).
If the dwelling was vacant > 60 consecutive days immediately before the loss, deny regardless of peril (except theft is excluded outright when vacant; all other perils denied under vacancy).

When the proximate cause cannot be established from the facts (genuinely ambiguous between a covered and an excluded peril), the examiner does NOT guess: the correct disposition is REFER (escalate for investigation), not APPROVE or DENY.

verdicts.APPROVE: Covered peril, no controlling exclusion; net payable > 0 after sub-limit and deductible.
verdicts.PARTIAL: Covered but reduced — by a sub-limit, by the deductible, or because only the ensuing portion is covered. Net payable > 0 but less than claimed.
verdicts.DENY: No covered peril, a controlling exclusion applies, vacancy override, or net payable <= 0 after deductible.
verdicts.REFER: Proximate cause genuinely indeterminate from the facts; escalate rather than guess (R7).
